A StopIteration exception leaking from the bowels of pdfrw up into user code is an _a priori_ indication that there is a bug in pdfrw.

The error you are now seeing is not like that.  It is telling you that (a) pdfrw found what appears to it to be a broken PDF file (the reported length of a stream doesn't match the actual length of the stream), and (b) it tried to fix it by adjusting the reported length to the actual length it found.  The fact you're still not satisfied probably also means that (c) this fix was insufficient and there are other issues either with the PDF or with pdfrw.

Without seeing the PDF itself, I cannot know whether this is a bug in pdfrw or a problem with the PDF, but literally (no, not figuratively, literally) millions of PDFs have been run through pdfrw, and it's usually pretty good about diagnosing things, the original StopIteration bug notwithstanding.

In fact, some of the examples that other users sent me that caused StopIteration were, in fact bad PDFs, and the other users were quite sensibly happy to get decent reporting about the issues with the actual PDFs rather than the unhelpful StopIteration exception, and the example where StopIteration occurred on a valid PDF was on a corner case PDF that (IMHO) no sane PDF writer program would actually produce.

In any case, I couldn't have found either of those bugs _without looking at the PDFs_, so no PDF, no diagnostic work, and if I am asked to diagnose a PDFs that pdfrw gives a reasonable error message on (such as the one you describe), my first question will be how you know that it is, in fact, a valid PDF.  Because a lot of them aren't.

Note that, just because acrobat or okular or whatever accepts the PDF, that doesn't actually mean the PDF is valid, because all of those engines have exception code to try to clean up PDFs that are broken in common ways.

Personally, I'm rather ambivalent about Postel's law; if somebody submits reasonable looking code that fixes broken PDFs without slowing down or doing the wrong thing with correct PDFs, I'll accept it, but I don't have the time or inclination to go out of my way to try to fix a particular good PDF that I don't even have a copy of, and if the PDF isn't in fact a valid PDF, somebody else will have to submit a patch to cope with it.

Note that there are PDF repair tools out there that usually do a pretty good job, and in a production environment, you could shell out to one of them to create a fixed copy of the PDF if pdfrw reports an exception on the PDF.

Regards,
Pat

Thanks for you feedback, Tom.

I haven't (yet) gotten to updating the examples with you os.pipe suggestion, which I think will be a nice approach. Instead I played around a bit with the client/server to exercise the implementation.

Unfortunatelly, I found serious limitations, which I noted on a comment in fd_client.py:
* Implementation fails client-side when a second remote call with a UNIX_FD argument is attempted. The behavior is as follows:
    * First remote call completes successfully.
    * Second remote call never reaches the server.
    * DBus closes the connection with the caller.
    * The server keeps running and responds correctly to subsequent calls from the next client run.
* Implementation fails server-side when handling a remote call with two UNIX_FD arguments. The behavior is as follows:
    * Client sends call message to DBus.
    * DBus fowards it to server.
    * Server fails with a struct.error exception in twisted/internet/unix.py.
    * Server is disconnected from DBus (not shure who initiates the disconnection, server or dbus).
    * Client fails, as expected, with org.freedesktop.DBus.Error.NoReply.

I'm currently a bit at a loss. DBus logs showed nothing and not being very familiar with dbus-monitor, all I can say is that in the second case, the two UNIX_FDs seem to be going through and look fine.

I hacked a bit of twisted/internet/unix.py:doRead, where the exception is raised, and realised that dbus is sending the UNIX_FDs in a format that isn't expected by Twisted (something along the lines of Twisted seems to expect one UNIX_FD per message, DBus seems to be sending both).

Maybe this (yet to be confirmed) different aproach at sending/receiving UNIX_FDs between Twisted and DBus also explains the first failure.

Other than recompiling DBus with DBUS_VERBOSE support -- which the one I'm running on Ubuntu Server 16.04 does not seem to have -- and examining DBus source code for the UNIX_FD sending/receiving implementation, I'm not sure how to further diagnose this.

Do you have any ideas?
Thanks in advance.

Presently, Windows is not the platform where development is focused. This is summary status of Windows support.
Packet dissecting (from pcap files):
Everything should work normally.
Packet crafting:
scapy < 3 uses libdnet to craft packets in Windows, but it is pain to install, and, especially, for python 3. winpcap (and winpcapy) has some limited support for sending packets. It is not well integrated in scapy3k yet, though it is planned. You can use send method of _Pcap_Wrapper_pypcap class. Any help on further developing this is welcome.
Packet sniffing:
Sniffing should work as is (assuming winpcap is working), but issues with wireless interface sniffing on Windows is a deal breaker for many people. Other tools also have major issues with wireless interface support. Any ideas how to move forward on this are welcome.

simple_map = {
    '*/1 * * * * * *': 'every 1 sec',
    '*/2 * * * * * *': 'every 2 sec',

for ex, speech in simple_map.items():

            print(simple_map[_])

    func = wrapped_func(ex)
    func.__name__ = ex
    crython.job(expr=ex)(func)

Can you upload the branch for Kappa? I'd like to see the code.

I agree that recording without any modification to the target would be ideal. If that's not possible or practical then I think only requiring actions on the `boto3.Session` object is 'good enough'. Sessions already need to be kept track of because they handle credentials, but as far as I know clients can be treated as stateless if they inherited their credentials from the session - they don't hold any data that you actually need to care about, they just implement the API methods. Putting the record/save/load actions on clients adds state to them, so now they have to be tracked and handled in ways that they might not have been before.

(Am I wrong in thinking clients can be treated as stateless? I'm not overly familiar with the inner workings of boto3 but I've never come across a reason to care about the 'contents' of a client object - I'd like to be able to maintain that assumption when adding Placebo to a project.)

Although unintuitive, values below 0 are in fact correct. The SSIM publications point out the unique maximum at 1 and skip over the part of a minimum value, so many people seem to just assume 0 (me included). It is only mentioned that the third term / the cross correlation can be negative. 

[Structural Approaches to Image Quality Assessment] (https://ece.uwaterloo.ca/~z70wang/publications/HIVP_chapter83.pdf) goes into most detail about this:

> Notice that s(x, y) can take on negative values. As will be shown in later examples, the negative structural similarity values correspond to the cases that the local image structures are inverted.

[Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Structural_similarity) mentions the bounds correctly (-1 to 1), this [OpenCV example](http://docs.opencv.org/2.4/doc/tutorials/gpu/gpu-basics-similarity/gpu-basics-similarity.html) does not (zero to one).

It's easy to reproduce an ssim value close to -1:

```
from numpy.random import randint
from PIL import Image
random_img = randint(0, 255, (1000, 1000)).astype(np.uint8)
print(compute_ssim(Image.fromarray(random_img), Image.fromarray(255 - random_img)))
# -0.965838768437 for me
```

Maybe the README of this project or the doc could mention that negative values are in fact correct and that the bounds are -1 and 1.

@jianghc724 Thanks for reporting this! I need to update the documentation. NLPIR requires bytes instead of unicode. So, here are two ways you can do it:
```python
pynlpir.nlpir.ImportUserDict(b'./data/dict.txt')
```
or
```python
pynlpir.nlpir.ImportUserDict('./data/dict.txt'.encode('utf-8'))
```

I'm not sure if NLPIR requires absolute filenames or not, so you might need to experiment with that.

I use [Seaborn](http://seaborn.pydata.org/) for plot styling. There are different "seaborn styles" that will change the background of your figure: http://seaborn.pydata.org/tutorial/aesthetics.html#seaborn-figure-styles

There is a config parameter in evo for exactly that. For example, if you want to change the style to `white`or `whitegrid`, just run:
```
evo_config set evo_config set plot_seaborn_style whitegrid
```

`evo_config show` will explain you some other parameters you can play around with. Changing some of the `plot_*` is probably what you want to do before printing.

Yeah I definitely think it's still valuable. You don't want to manually run your test suite on each supported version of python, right? I assume you're mainly using python 3 yourself, so it doesn't look like the new stuff you committed would have caught the python 2 error that went out. 

You can even have travis build for a large list of specific python releases. Like you certainly don't want to manually run your tests on FIFTEEN different pythons, but travis could do that easily if you want to make sure you don't break on any of those versions.

A hook to block the release if the travis build fails is nice to have, but it's not really necessary. You can just take a look at it yourself before a release as a sanity check. In fact, it probably wouldn't even get that far, because the little red X shown in the github commit history UI would probably make someone notice it shortly after whatever commit introduces a problem. you can also set up email notifications for when a build starts failing, in case you never ever look at the github history UI.

Thanks for reporting. The issue here is that multiinter does not output an actual BED file, it's a report of the various intersections.

```python
import pybedtools as bt
import pandas

bedfiles = ["a.bed", "b.bed"]
x = bt.BedTool()
bfl = []
for bf in bedfiles:
    bfl.append(bt.example_bedtool(bf))

intsect = x.multi_intersect(i=[b.fn for b in bfl], header=True)
df = pandas.read_table(intsect.fn)
```
See also https://github.com/daler/pybedtools/issues/113; I'll add a note to the docs for `multi_inter` as a reminder it doesn't give BED output.

I also recommend against the following pattern when using the ansible backend.  This will create a connection and execute `crm_resource list` for every entry of the list .  In this case, it's faster to run `crm_resource list` once, and assert the result is what I expect.

I was unaware of this until recently, and thought I would share.  This small change sped up our tests by 2/3rds.

```
@pytest.mark.parametrize('resource_group, resource', [
    ('admin-gateway-group', 'admin_gateway_ip'),
    ('admin-gateway-group', 'admin_snat_ip'),
    ('repository-vip-group', 'repository_vip'),
    ('haproxy-vip-group', 'haproxy_service_network_vip'),
    ('haproxy-vip-group', 'haproxy_admin_network_vip'),
    ('rsyslog-vip-group', 'rsyslog_vip'), ('percona-vip-group', 'percona_vip'),
    ('memcache-vip-group', 'memcache_vip'),
    ('neutron-dhcp-agent-group', 'neutron-dhcp-agent'),
    ('zabbix-proxy-vip-group', 'zabbix_proxy_vip')
])
def test_crm_resource_groups(Command, resource_group, resource):
    cmd = 'crm_resource list'
    out = Command.check_output(cmd)

    assert re.search(
        r'Resource Group: {}.*{}.*Started'.format(resource_group, resource),
        out, re.DOTALL)
```

Yes, lasio is considering 999.25 as a NULL in your case because of the default value for null_policy (``lasio.read(..., null_policy='common')`` - see the [documentation](http://lasio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/data-section.html#handling-invalid-data-indicators-automatically)). If you use ``null_policy='strict'`` that will fix your problem.

From a larger perspective this is tricky to fix because the replacements are done before wrapping is applied - see https://github.com/kinverarity1/lasio/blob/master/lasio/reader.py#L328. If I choose to ignore null substitutions for the first column, that's easy enough, but will only work for unwrapped files. I'd certainly welcome a PR that ignores all substitutions for the true first column in both wrapped and unwrapped files!

After experimenting with MS access databases, I found that UnicodeDecodeError only seems to occur for me when a column contains a description (labelled *Description (optional)* in Access). Your mileage may vary, but a test goes as:

1. Create a blank database with a table 'x' and a single column (of any kind) with no description.
2. Try the following code, adding a character to the description each time you run:

```python
import pyodbc
with pyodbc.connect(
    dbq='pyodbc.connect(dbq='N:/test.accdb',
    driver='Microsoft Access Driver (*.mdb, *.accdb)') as db:
    for i in db.cursor().columns('x'):
        desc, c, rest = i[11].partition('\x00')
        print(desc, list(bytes(c + rest, 'utf8')))
```
Your mileage may vary with the format you get, but it appears to me as if Access adds on the same amount of junk bytes as there are characters in the description. Certain descriptions simply have bytes so high that they're illegible under UTF-16.

@bergerod: does the error go away if you remove descriptions from the columns of the table you're doing `Cursor.columns` on?

This error will occur if you try to run PyODBC under tox on Windows, and its because tox is actually suppressing the ``WINDIR`` environment variable. You can just pass that variable through and this error will go away.

Hi @AnneTheAgile,

sorry for the delay of this reply. 

For your first question about the distinct output: The `mloginfo --distinct` code parses the entire mongo codebase for occurrences of `log()` or `LOG()` or some other functions/macros that output log lines. It then goes through these lines and collects strings, which it will use to identify a given log line. Unfortunately, the process is quite complex, because the code that outputs log lines can have variables in them. It looks like there is no matching line in the code that has the literal `"connections:"` string.

```
$ git grep "connections:"
src/mongo/util/net/message_server_port.cpp:79:                log() << "connection refused because too many open connections: " << Listener::globalTicketHolder.used() << endl;
```

Therefore `--distinct` can't match the line. Adding these manually would be too time-consuming.

As for [mongodb-log](https://github.com/imlucas/mongodb-log), this project was started by one of my colleagues and an intern. I've been contributing to it as well, with the goal of having a similar log parsing library in Javascript. It doesn't have the full functionality of mtools' log parser yet, but it's getting close and is definitely already usable. 

Hope this answers your questions.

Thomas

Hmm. The problem is a circular import issue. The import of `keyrings.alt.file` is triggering an import of `keyring`, which is initializing the entry points, which is triggering an import of `keyrings.alt.Windows`, which is trying to import `keyrings.alt.file`... and failing because file is incomplete in being imported. Prior to the move of these deprecated backends to `keyrings.alt`, this wasn't an issue because the entry points would get loaded on the invocation of `import keyring`.

I suspect you can work around the issue by issuing `import keyring` before your import... though you shouldn't have to do that. It's not obvious to me what the solution is, though, so I'll have to circle back on this issue later.

OK, I've reworked the patch and I think I've fixed the checks for the needed functionality while also making this change more general by eliminating the use of `type()` altogether.  The `type()` function only determines the immediate type of the given object and does not even match derived types.  This means that e.g. the recently added support for callables does not actually support callables such as class and object methods or callable classes implementing `__call__` but only plain functions.  A more pythonic approach is to use `isinstance()` where necessary (e.g. in order to distinguish strings from other sequences) or to check for the required functionality either by simply using them and catching exceptions or by using `isinstance()` in combination with abstract base classes which allow for convenient checking for certain groups of functionality.  This makes it possible for library consumers to pass in their custom data types as long as they implement the required functionality without tediously converting them to the exact types the chevron code is explicitly checking for.

First of all, I apologise for the terminology that I will use in my explanation below:
The _check_socket method will only detect the disconnection when the disconnection happened at the pymysensors side (very rare I think).
If the gateway for example is suddenly turned off, pymysensors will not detect the disconnection until it tries to send something to it.
Imagine a gateway with only sensors attached to it (temperature sensors for example) that only send data to pymysensors. If the gateway goes down for a couple of minutes, the pymysensors will never detect it because it is just waiting to receive data. Only when pymysensors tries to send something it will realise that the connection was lost and then close the socket. (The socket will still be alive until something is sent). After the socket is closed then it will try to connect again.
This is my understanding about how it works and because of that reason I thought about sending something (Version request) every x seconds to the gateway.

The question is: "how limited?"  I just pushed some new code to GitHub that will hopefully fix the problem you have encountered.  Please let me know.  There are other options if this fails.

A few details: Pandoc does not supply its version number to filters (see [Issue 2640](https://github.com/jgm/pandoc/issues/2640).  This causes challenges when version-dependent processing needs to be done.  I thought I had a solution in asking the filter's parent process (i.e., pandoc) for version information.  This has worked for others, but not on readthedocs.

The new code falls back to calling the `pandoc` command.  This should work fine for most users, but not for me.  I need the psutil capabilities because I have multiple pandoc executables (i.e., pandoc-1.17.0.2, etc) on my machine for testing purposes.

What I want is the ability to have Hass manage "Smart Polling" to be much friendlier to the car battery: While the vehicle is driving, polling will be fairly rapid, but when the car is parked and not charging, polling will step down gradually to be quite infrequent, to preserve the battery. And if the battery gets to a critical state, such as 20% or less, polling will step down even further. Another objective here is that I will be exposing Hass services that can force a vehicle update, and another that will force and update and reset the step-down timer. Hi @whzup !

Awesome questions! Made me review my robotics basics again. :laughing:  So, for a Denavit Hartenberg (D-H) transformation matrix, the values in the fourth column (we start indexing by 0) describes the [translation from axis x to axis y (Check the last sentence in this link)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Denavit%E2%80%93Hartenberg_parameters#Denavit%E2%80%93Hartenberg_matrix).  That's why we are only taking those values.

Imagine your elbow joint as axis x and your wrist joint as axis y. If you want to know how your *wrist joint translates with respect to your elbow joint*, you compute the D-H matrix by doing a dot-product:

```
import numpy as np
transform_matrix_from_elbow_to_wrist = np.dot(elbow_joint, wrist_joint)
```

The result is a 4 x 4 matrix that we now know as the D-H transformation matrix. But what if we want to know how the *end-tip of our index finger translates with respect to our elbow joint*? Then, we successively compute the transformation matrix from elbow to wrist, then wrist to index finger tip. Same reason why there 's a succession of `np.dot`s to obtain `Etip`.

> I'm guessing that Etip is the transformation matrix for the end tip so that if you multiply it by a vector the position of the end tip would be the result, or am I wrong with that

In hindsight, I think that my variable name is misleading. You are right that the Etip is the transformation matrix from the zeroth-axis to the end-tip. Although I am not sure about multiplying a vector to find the end-tip position. Usually we just obtain the three values in the fourth column.

There are a few different options.

First, there is the NullBar which can be used as a drop-in replacement for the regular ProgressBar but it won't print anything: http://progressbar-2.readthedocs.io/en/latest/progressbar.bar.html?highlight=nullbar#progressbar.bar.NullBar

Second, you can pass a "fake" file descriptor to the progress bar so the output will never be rendered, but that will still execute the progressbar in the background so I wouldn't recommend that solution.

For your code you could do something like this:

```python
if production:
    ProgressBar = progressbar.NullBar()
else:
    ProgressBar = progressbar.ProgressBar()
```

> Thanks for the clarification! Out of curiosity at what point could it happen that meta doesn't exist on an object?


Objects always have `meta`, but if we call `bulk_update` with a custom `meta`, the `meta` from the objects is ignored and we use the `meta` from the arguments for all of them.

In other words:
`bulk_update` has an optional argument called `meta`. If `meta` is not None we will use it to update all objects in `objs`.
But if we don't have this "global" `meta`, we will update each object using its own `meta`.

The following config variables were necessary:

    # override the flask-security config for fast password hashes
    flask_app.config['SECURITY_HASHING_SCHEMES'] = ['plaintext']
    flask_app.config['SECURITY_DEPRECATED_HASHING_SCHEMES'] = []

And the following also gives a minor and useful speed bump too:

    flask_app.config['SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH'] = 'plaintext'

Looks like we're using `field.value_from_object(instance)` because it doesn't traverse foreignkeys, but it appears it *does* traverse `ManyToManyField`. Because this happens in `post_init` signal, the `CustomUser` object doesn't exist in the database yet, so the M2M can't be traversed.

I think, it should probably just assume the value is `[]` in this scenario rather than try to traverse the M2M. However it should also potentially handle saved objects better, by using the related objects cache before trying to traverse the M2M.

Interesting. 

Looks like `OneToOneRel` is the reverse side of a `OneToOneField`. It's a pseudo-field which is stored on a different model.

I think this falls into the same bucket as ManyToManyField (#3) - changes could happen by updating a related model, so it makes change detection difficult.

I think the best solution for now is to not support this field, and exclude it when automatically selecting fields to track.

I had the same problem. Here is the solution:
`    def __ext_snipplet(self, name):
        """
        Render another docx body into my document
        """
        document = DocxTemplate(self.getSnippletPath(name))
        document.render(self.jinjaContext)

        # Remove the sectPr element ("SecrionProperties")
        # "This element defines the section properties for the final section of the document." (Source: https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/documentformat.openxml.wordprocessing.sectionproperties(v=office.14).aspx)
        # This is not needed in case of a snipplet
        if document._element.body.sectPr is not None:
            document._element.body.remove(document._element.body.sectPr)

        # Remove the body xml tag
        xml = re.sub(r'</?w:body[^>]*>', '', document.get_xml())

        return xml`

It is really important to remove the "sectPr" Element and the body open and close tags before inserting another document into yours.
More information are provided in the comments of the code.

1. create a new abstract class EventCalendar()
2. the EventCalendar() class has methods for:
2a) "regular_events()" where that is a tuple of pandas AbstractHolidayClass that defines the event days and a datetime of the time of the event.
2b) "adhoc_events()" which is a list of dicts, where the key is the regular event date and the value is the datetime of the event. This is for events that sometimes move to non-standard dates like non-farm payrolls when they would fall on an early close date
2c) the "schedule()" method would now take a start and end time where that defined the window around the event that you want excluded.

Now that we have the date_range of times we want to exclude, another function "merge_market_and_events" (needs a better name) that takes a date_range from the MarketCalendar and a date_range from the EventCalendar. 1.) In a production environment, we'd want more than 1 worker to handle more requests concurrently. Maybe people are running/updating thousands of jobs per minute, or maybe we've extended our Flask views such that the server does some other things, besides just manage CRUD of these jobs.

2.) This pull requests does not fix the issue, at all. Apologies. This was done before properly reading Gunicorn documentation. You are correct in saying that each worker is a new OS process.
 ______________________________________________________________________________________________________
All this considered, if I run the Gunicorn server with the *--preload* flag, the app will load before the workers (processes) are forked. As a result, each worker gets an identical copy of the APScheduler instance, the difference being the Thread created in start() never gets created in the workers, so they never schedule jobs.

**As long as we choose a jobstore other than :memory:, (i.e. sqlalchemy)** I believe all CRUD operations concerning Jobs can be handled by **any** worker. The reason this is possible is that although each worker has an APScheduler instance without the BackgroundScheduler's thread running, it inherits the state=STATE_RUNNING from it's master Process when it is forked. 

So when we add a new job, rather than put the job into the **"pending_jobs"** list due to STATE_STOPPED, we simply create this job on the SQLite jobstore. This is a hack, but it works!

So we use this all the time. We leverage the Variables as config, and so
getting sane defaults into the DB is very helpful for us and makes
migration from Dev->Qa->Prod much more stable.

There were a few comments about how this is a bad idea, and a get should
never set a value, but as we already support defaults, giving a way to put
those in the DB makes our system more stable. Basically, you get to see
what the default is, and edit it, instead of trying to remember them.

We currently handle this in our helper object, but it is MUCH cleaner to
add them. Basically, it adds an extra parameter to the Variable.Get and if
there is a default AND the extra param is set, the default is written to
the DB if the value doesn't exist. This allows us to see, when looking at
the Variable screen, what the supported variables are.

Just makes things more readable in a real world ops environment.

Thanks,
Ben

This should be relatively easy to do. Basically all you have to do is comply with [this interface](https://github.com/mozilla/sops/blob/master/sops.go#L244-L250). You can see how we do this for json [here](https://github.com/mozilla/sops/blob/master/json/store.go#L183-L256).

The main difference here is that as you said .properties files don't have maps or sequences, and sops's [`Metadata`](https://github.com/mozilla/sops/blob/master/sops.go#L219-L226) is something like a map, and it contains a sequence. For this reason marshaling and unmarshaling `Metadata` is going to be the most complex part. Otherwise, everything else can be parsed onto a simple `TreeBranch`, which is just a slice (array) of `TreeItem`s, which are simple key-values.

If you decide to give it a shot, don't hesitate to ping me for questions.

- Example after this PR: 
```Python
from traitlets import *
class Foo(HasTraits):
    pass
foo = Foo()
foo.add_trait('bar', Int(1)) 
print(foo.bar )  # prints 1 because self.init_default_value is called in `__get__`.
```

**Problem** 

Any un-parseable `Expires` header will mean you cannot retrieve the object with `boto3`.

**Proposed Solution**

In `botocore.parsers.ResponseParser`, `_handle_timestamp` should return `None` or, just the original text if it cannot be converted to datetime. (Possibly wrapped in custom type, ie, `Unparseable(data)`)

**Details**

@kyleknap Here's a reproduction from an internal issue we had with migrating from boto to boto3.

For some versions of `boto`, it would urlencode any `unicode` types passed in`headers`, resulting in objects that cannot be retrieved by `boto3`.

It's not present in `2.45.0`, but I can confirm it is present in `boto==2.38.0`


```py
import boto, six, datetime, os
from boto.s3.key import Key

bucket_name = 'my-bucket-to-test'
key_name = os.urandom(8).encode('hex')
data = os.urandom(16)
hdr_expires = u'Tue, 24 Jan 2017 07:52:32 GMT' # this being a unicode string causes the issue

conn = boto.connect_s3()
bucket = conn.get_bucket(bucket_name, validate=False)
key = bucket.new_key(key_name)
key.set_contents_from_file(
    six.BytesIO(data),
    headers={'Expires': hdr_expires})

# Wont expose the Expires header without subclass
class Key2(Key):
    def __init__(self, bucket=None, name=None):
        super(Key2, self).__init__(bucket=bucket, name=name)
        self._expires_header = {}
    def handle_addl_headers(self, headers):
        for k, v in headers:
            if k == 'expires':
                self._expires_header = v

out = six.BytesIO()
fetched = Key2(bucket, key_name)
fetched.get_contents_to_file(out)
print 'Boto2 Expires: %r' % fetched._expires_header

```

with `boto==2.38.0` this prints
```
Boto2 Expires: 'Tue,%2024%20Jan%202017%2007:52:32%20GMT'
```

with `boto==2.45.0` this prints
```
Boto2 Expires: 'Tue, 24 Jan 2017 07:52:32 GMT'
```

The result - objects affected by that bug cannot be read by `boto3`.

    """

    from json import loads
    status_code = None

    for log in browser.driver.get_log('performance'):

Here is a sample to show how to create a Tag,  a Tag Category and tag a Cluster using basic auth(username/password). The sample skips the server certificate verification for simplicity sake which should be avoided in production code:

```python
import ssl
import time

import requests
from com.vmware.cis.tagging_client import (
    Category, Tag, TagAssociation, CategoryModel)
from com.vmware.cis_client import Session
from com.vmware.vapi.std_client import DynamicID
from pyVim import connect
from pyVmomi import vim
from vmware.vapi.lib.connect import get_requests_connector
from vmware.vapi.security.session import create_session_security_context
from vmware.vapi.security.user_password import \
    create_user_password_security_context
from vmware.vapi.stdlib.client.factories import StubConfigurationFactory

server = '<server_ip>'
url = 'https://{}/api'.format(server)
username = '<username>'
password = '<password>'
category_name = 'sample category'
category_desc = 'sample category description'
tag_name = 'sample tag'
tag_desc = 'sample tag description'
cluster_name = 'cluster'


def create_tag_category(name, description, cardinality):
    """create a category. User who invokes this needs create category privilege."""
    create_spec = category_svc.CreateSpec()
    create_spec.name = name
    create_spec.description = description
    create_spec.cardinality = cardinality
    associableTypes = set()
    create_spec.associable_types = associableTypes
    return category_svc.create(create_spec)


def delete_tag_category(category_id):
    """Deletes an existing tag category; User who invokes this API needs
    delete privilege on the tag category.
    """
    category_svc.delete(category_id)


def create_tag(name, description, category_id):
    """Creates a Tag"""
    create_spec = tag_svc.CreateSpec()
    create_spec.name = name
    create_spec.description = description
    create_spec.category_id = category_id
    return tag_svc.create(create_spec)


def update_tag(tag_id, description):
    """Update the description of an existing tag.
    User who invokes this API needs edit privilege on the tag.
    """
    update_spec = tag_svc.UpdateSpec()
    update_spec.setDescription = description
    tag_svc.update(tag_id, update_spec)


def delete_tag(tag_id):
    """Delete an existing tag.
    User who invokes this API needs delete privilege on the tag."""
    tag_svc.delete(tag_id)


def get_cluster_id(name):
    """Find cluster id by given name using pyVmomi."""
    context = None
    if hasattr(ssl, '_create_unverified_context'):
        context = ssl._create_unverified_context()

    si = connect.Connect(host=server, user=username, pwd=password,
                         sslContext=context)
    content = si.content
    container = content.rootFolder
    viewType = [vim.ClusterComputeResource]
    recursive = True
    clusterView = content.viewManager.CreateContainerView(container,
                                                          viewType,
                                                          recursive)
    clusters = clusterView.view
    for cluster in clusters:
        if cluster.name == name:
            return cluster._GetMoId()
    raise Exception('Cluster with name {} could not be found'.format(name))


"""
Create an authenticated stub configuration object that can be used to issue
requests against vCenter.
Returns a stub_config that stores the session identifier that can be used
to issue authenticated requests against vCenter.
"""
session = requests.Session()
session.verify = False
connector = get_requests_connector(session=session, url=url)
stub_config = StubConfigurationFactory.new_std_configuration(connector)

# Pass user credentials (user/password) in the security context to authenticate.
# login to vAPI endpoint
user_password_security_context = create_user_password_security_context(username,
                                                                       password)
stub_config.connector.set_security_context(user_password_security_context)

# Create the stub for the session service and login by creating a session.
session_svc = Session(stub_config)
session_id = session_svc.create()

# Successful authentication.  Store the session identifier in the security
# context of the stub and use that for all subsequent remote requests
session_security_context = create_session_security_context(session_id)
stub_config.connector.set_security_context(session_security_context)

# Create Tagging services
tag_svc = Tag(stub_config)
category_svc = Category(stub_config)
tag_association = TagAssociation(stub_config)

print('List all the existing categories user has access to...')
categories = category_svc.list()
if len(categories) > 0:
    for category in categories:
        print('Found Category: {0}'.format(category))
else:
    print('No Tag Category Found...')

print('List all the existing tags user has access to...')
tags = tag_svc.list()
if len(tags) > 0:
    for tag in tags:
        print('Found Tag: {0}'.format(tag))
else:
    print('No Tag Found...')

print('creating a new tag category...')
category_id = create_tag_category(category_name,
                                  category_desc,
                                  CategoryModel.Cardinality.MULTIPLE)
assert category_id is not None
print('Tag category created; Id: {0}'.format(category_id))

print("creating a new Tag...")
tag_id = create_tag(tag_name, tag_desc, category_id)
assert tag_id is not None
print('Tag created; Id: {0}'.format(tag_id))

print('updating the tag...')
date_time = time.strftime('%d/%m/%Y %H:%M:%S')
update_tag(tag_id, 'Server Tag updated at ' + date_time)
print('Tag updated; Id: {0}'.format(tag_id))

print('finding the cluster {0}'.format(cluster_name))
cluster_moid = get_cluster_id(cluster_name)
assert cluster_moid is not None
print('Found cluster:{0} mo_id:{1}'.format('vAPISDKCluster', cluster_moid))

print('Tagging the cluster {0}...'.format(cluster_name))
dynamic_id = DynamicID(type='ClusterComputeResource', id=cluster_moid)
tag_association.attach(tag_id=tag_id, object_id=dynamic_id)
for tag_id in tag_association.list_attached_tags(dynamic_id):
    if tag_id == tag_id:
        tag_attached = True
        break
assert tag_attached
print('Tagged cluster: {0}'.format(cluster_moid))
```

@magianxd You are right, It looks like the connectionPooltimeout parameter only close idle connections from the client side, and if the client connection was idle longer than the timeout value, a new connection will be created automatically. 

How many connections you created using SmartConnect? I suspect your timeout issue is due to the server close the connection because the session times out. This is controlled on server side and is configurable via server settings. See https://kb.vmware.com/selfservice/microsites/search.do?language=en_US&cmd=displayKC&externalId=1017253.

Another option is to try SessionOrientedStub which will re-login if the previous session times out. 

I think PR #493 was valid. We just add the support to pass the customized connectionPooltimeout to SoapStubAdapter. Do you see any issues with it?

It is however fixed in master and will be released shortly.  The behavior should be as follows =>

* You pin the new version of (or unpin) whatever package you want in your `Pipfile`
* You run `Pipenv update`
* Pipenv runs `pipenv lock` as a result in order to make sure that your change is also OK with the rest of the packages you have installed
* Pipenv then runs `pipenv sync` to sync the new lockfile with your environment

> I noticed that there is no librdkafka solution for balanced consumers

That's because those should go the way of the dodo bird. Much better to use Kafka's native Consumer Group API's which are supported by `librdkafka` and also exposed by `pykafka` as `ManagedBalancedConsumers`. Pykafka's `BalancedConsumer` was built before these native Kafka API's existed, and (IMHO based on our production usage) has fairly serious problems such as https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/issues/354

Ok, I was thinking about this a bit longer, and I agree this pullreq takes the right approach by removing the exception. If you have n partitions, and you add n+1 consumers, one of them is going to be partitionless (and you can't predict which one because they are sorted by their randomly picked IDs) and there shouldn't be any problems as a result of that.

But then if that's not going to raise exceptions, I think that means the consumer shouldn't `stop()` (so basically that means all the `should_stop` logic would disappear from `_rebalance()`). Revisiting #313 I now think the main issue there was just that a partitionless consumer was "unstoppable", not that it should stop automatically.

So I think we would be ok if we're "watering down" `test_no_partitions()`: if you have a consumer that doesn't hold any partitions, the test should just check that `consume()` returns `None` and that a call to `stop()` won't fail.

My thoughts are something like this:

Handle extra type of exception and add the appropriate message in front of the particular key. In the below example, key's value is represented same like this.

```
fire: <module 'fire' from '/Users/srpatel/github/python-fire/fire/__init__.pyc'>
x:    {'y': {...}}
```

The issue of `__main__` namespace getting mangled is pretty big, e.g., it makes pickling really annoying and sometimes causes weird issues with imports.

Just to clarify what I mean about namespaces:

```
fire -m pandas -- --interactive
IPython shell!
$ read_csv('whatever')
$ import pandas as pd
$ assert read_csv is pd.read_csv 
$ assert DataFrame.__module__ != '__main__'
```

Under the hood would be essentially the same as what you have, but using import_module instead.

```
import importlib
module = importlib.import_module(name)
fire.Fire(module)
```

Which would then cause all of the module's locals (or maybe `__dict__` to be in namespace).

So then you could end up with something like:

```
fire -m numpy genfromtxt 'myfile.csv' --delimiter=',' - sum
```

Sort of workaround for using `captureException` outside try/except block:

```python
import sys

import raven


def get_exc_info():
    exc_info = sys.exc_info()
    if exc_info[0] is None:
        return None
    return exc_info


def capture_exception(error)
    sentry = raven.Client(...)
    exc_info = get_exc_info()
    if exc_info:
        sentry.captureException(exc_info)
    else:
        sentry.captureMessage(error)
```

```
import numpy
from neurom import fst

def compute_bounding_box(neuron):
    bboxes = numpy.array([fst._mm.bounding_box(n) for n in neuron.neurites])
    _min = bboxes[:,0]
    _max = bboxes[:,1]
    _min = numpy.min(_min, axis=0)

```
setup_v1.py:
	cp -R tower_cli tower_cli_v1/
	cp bin/tower-cli bin/tower-cli-v1
	cp setup.py setup_v1.py
	python version_swap.py

prep_v1: setup_v1.py

install_v1: setup_v1.py
	sudo rm -rf dist/ build/
	sudo python setup_v1.py install
```
The idea here is that if you do `make install_v1` in a fresh checkout, it will do the prep steps rather than just failing. If you do it a second time, it will notice the existence of `setup_v1.py` and avoid re-executing the prep steps.

Hi!  Thanks for this PR.  This fix is more appropriate for CPython (where the [problem originates](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Python/Python-ast.c#L4652)) rather than typed_ast.  typed_ast is based off the CPython parser and doesn't accept bug or style fixes for the CPython code, as it aims for bug-for-bug compatibility and every additional change makes it harder to update onto new versions of CPython.  That said, this shadowing does seem unfortunate -- I'd encourage you to submit a fix to CPython instead (which typed_ast will get eventually when we the version of the CPython parser our code is based on).

Another important thing to note: `Python-ast.c` is autogenerated from [`asdl_c.py`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Parser/asdl_c.py) -- you'll need to make the fix in that file as well.

Let's assume that you have a backup of your cluster and archive logs on S3.

What Patroni can do with it:
1. It can create a new replica in your exiting cluster with the help of `patroni_wale_restore` (it will make some decisions whether to get backup from s3 or handover to basebackup). And [here](https://github.com/zalando/spilo/blob/master/postgres-appliance/configure_spilo.py#L244) you can take an example of configuration for patroni_wale_restore.
2. With the help of custom bootstrap you can create a _new_ Patroni cluster, restored either up to the last available wal segment, to specific transaction or to a specific time (PITR) and `patroni_wale_restore` can' do it. You have to write your own script for that. Here is an example how we did it: https://github.com/zalando/spilo/blob/master/postgres-appliance/bootstrap/clone_with_s3.py

And [here](https://github.com/zalando/spilo/blob/master/postgres-appliance/configure_spilo.py#L110) you can have a look how it could be configured.

Got it even more concise - rather than deleting specific properties, you can use jq to only use specific properties. I've compiled the 3 commands to copy organizations, groups, and datasets and have the datasets be associated with the organizations and groups:

To copy the organizations
```bash
$ ckanapi dump organizations --all -q -r https://opendataphilly.org \
   | jq --compact-output '{name, title, description}' \
   | ckanapi load organizations -c $CKAN_INI
```

To copy the groups/topics
```bash
$ ckanapi dump groups --all -q -r https://opendataphilly.org \
   | jq --compact-output '{name, title, description}' \
   | ckanapi load groups -c $CKAN_INI
```

To copy the datasets
```bash
$ ckanapi dump datasets --all -q -r https://opendataphilly.org \
   | jq --compact-output '{name, title, notes, license_id, license_title, maintainer, maintainer_email, owner_org: .organization.name, extras, groups: [.groups[] | {name}], resources: [.resources[] | {url, name, format, description}]}' \
   | ckanapi load datasets -c $CKAN_INI
```

the performance penalty will also depend on the particular expressions involved. migration involves a tree traversal throughout the expression, creating new objects for, at worst, every expression in the tree. so naturally larger expression objects will take "longer" to migrate.

hm, you're only timing how long it takes to create the generator, not how long it takes to exhaust it. 

```
assert list(crle(x)) == list(irle(x))
# Creating the generator
%timeit crle(x)  # 100000 loops, best of 3: 10.1 µs per loop
%timeit irle(x)  # 100000 loops, best of 3: 3.22 µs per loop
# Iterating over the generator
%timeit list(crle(x))  # 1 loop, best of 3: 228 ms per loop
%timeit list(irle(x))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 103 ms per loop
```

However if you want `partitionby` to be faster than replace the `lambda x: x` with `None`:

```
crle = lambda xs: ((ilen(gp), gp[0]) for gp in cz.recipes.partitionby(None, xs))

assert list(crle(x)) == list(irle(x))
%timeit list(crle(x))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 164 ms per loop
%timeit list(irle(x))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 104 ms per loop
```

At this point the bottleneck is that numpy-arrays are slow when iterated over even with `itertools`, there are some ways to make it faster, for example converting it to a `list`:

```
%timeit list(crle(x.tolist()))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 126 ms per loop
%timeit list(irle(x.tolist()))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 67.8 ms per loop
``` 

The time difference here is mostly due to the fact that `groupby` doesn't need to create intermediate sequences before counting them.  So the next step would be to use normal `len` in `partitionby`:

```
crle = lambda xs: ((len(gp), gp[0]) for gp in cz.recipes.partitionby(None, xs))
%timeit list(crle(x.tolist()))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 89.4 ms per loop
%timeit list(irle(x.tolist()))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 79.1 ms per loop
```

Still a bit slower but the difference isn't all that much anymore.

You're working with homogeneous array-data, so you should consider switching to a numpy-based solution instead of working in pure-Python (or using PyPy?). Just to give an example using `numba` (just some draft, I don't think that function is really stable):

```
import numba as nb

@nb.njit
def counts(arr):
    res = np.zeros((arr.size, 2), dtype=np.int32)
    i = 0
    cnts = 0
    last = None
    for item in arr:
        if last is None:
            last = item
        if item == last:
            cnts += 1
        else:
            res[i, 0] = cnts
            res[i, 1] = last
            last = item
            cnts = 1
            i += 1
    res[i, 0] = cnts
    res[i, 1] = last
    i += 1
    return res[:i]

[tuple(inner) for inner in counts(x).tolist()] == list(crle(x.tolist()))
%timeit list(crle(x.tolist()))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 89.9 ms per loop
%timeit list(irle(x.tolist()))  # 10 loops, best of 3: 77.4 ms per loop
%timeit counts(x)  # 1000 loops, best of 3: 854 µs per loop
```

So this can speed up you function by a factor of 100.

I'm not sure what the takeaway message of this comment should be: I think `partitionby` is really useful because it's much easier to work with than `groupby`. However where `groupby` has it's weird quirks it can be faster than `partitionby`. But the bottom line should probably be: If you're working with numeric and homogeneous data and you **need performance**, then you probably should stay in the `numpy`-domain (maybe `pandas`).

To be clear the motivation is to keep this a part of the router, but disable it by default. There have been issues with SSL redirection with proxy protocol enabled because certain headers typically populated by the ELB like `http_x_forwarded_proto` were not being sent, which is what SSL redirection relies on.

If you still want websocket support you'll just have to run `deisctl config router set proxy protocol=1` to enable it and everything is all honkey-dory. If you're upgrading from an older cluster this setting was already enabled so the upgrade path is not broken with this change either. This just turns it off by default so users who are aware of this feature can enable it, but it won't hurt others working with other nginx modules. Makes sense? :)

> Yes, once you have broadcast a transaction, the only effective way to replace it is with a transaction that has a higher gas price (by 10%, in most default implementations). Mining nodes will just ignore lower priced transaction broadcasts (why not, they get more fees for accepting your earlier broadcast transaction 😄 ).

That makes sense from a convenience perspective but I wonder if considering this in the API itself is necessary. For me, I would expect replacing a transaction to work as any other transaction, i.e. i provide the parameters and the node receives it. Now, if I provided a parameter that miners didn't like then as the consumer of the API, I should understand and handle that. That means that if miner expectations of these parameters change for whatever reason in the future then apps can adapt.

Hope i'm not being too pedantic here. I guess its a question of how much convenience vs flexibility this api wants to provide.

To follow up, this is the "fix" that has worked for me

  pip install requests==2.7.0
  conda install -y requests=2.7.0

It downgrades the requests package.

Note that the error is produced when running the "conda" command,  I found empirically that conda won't do anything as long as the requests package is broken in this way.  By using "pip" first, the broken package can be downgraded.  Then, the "conda" command is run to ensure that the downgrade is complete.  Tedious but it works.

After this, conda is working again.

You don't need to use channel alias for that.  Channel alias is only used when you don't fully specify the channel.  You can fully specify the channel doing:  https://my.repo/admin in the "channels" key.  

Also, you will want to use "default_channels": http://conda.pydata.org/docs/install/central.html#default-channels-default-channels

So i recommend something like this, because if you ever create a ~/.condarc with channels, "defaults" is added automatically and it will expand to the public repos.  It's good practice to re-assign the "defaults" shortcut if you never want to hit the public repos.

```
channel_alias: https://my.repo/
channels:
  - defaults

default_channels:
  - https://my.repo/admin
```

And run `anaconda config --set url "http://myrepo"`.

@mrocklin my main scientific motivation is the desire to push the limits of interactive genetic data analysis. The genetic data I typically work with is very rich and needs to be explored from many different angles to build understanding of data quality issues and to discover interesting signals in the data.

Some analyses are compute heavy and will always interrupt the flow, however a lot of work also goes into preparation, exploration and visualisation of data which is less compute heavy, and where I/O can be as costly as compute. In these cases, if lots of steps in the workflow each take minutes rather than seconds to compute, it very much interrupts the flow and limits the number of avenues that can be explored.

The current scale of the data is ~100 million features by ~1000 observations which is not actually that big, with compressed arrays and chunked algorithms it is perfectly possible to do interactive analysis on a commodity laptop or desktop. However, the costs of genome sequencing are coming down very quickly, and we expect to be working with datasets 10 times this size within the next couple of years.

The main limitation in my analysis code right now is the fact that all computations are done in series. My desktop is quite modern and has 8 cores and it pains me to sit there for minutes waiting for a computation to finish and watching only 1 of 8 CPUs being used. I think dask is a great way for me to start parallelising a lot of the work I do. I particularly like the threaded scheduler, because it doesn't incur any of the overhead or complexity of working with multiple processes. I.e., it is very simple to set up and use, and fits very nicely with interactive analysis on a single machine. 

I do have bigger machines available but that won't help if I'm not able to effectively utilise multiple cores. Also I'm keen to push what is possible on commodity machines because it makes this type of data analysis so much more accessible to more people. With a laptop and a 2T hard disk I can sit on a train and get all kinds of things done. I also have access to a compute cluster with a job scheduler (SGE) so I can do big things in parallel if needed, but there is a lot of overhead that comes with coding things to work against a job scheduler and also monitoring and debugging results, so another reason to push what's possible on a single machine with multiple cores.

Sorry that's a bit vague, but it really has been transformative for me and my colleagues to be able to work interactively with the data we currently have, which is mainly down to efficient data structures (numpy, HDF5 and bcolz). If we can start parallelising some work via dask I think that will further accelerate current work and put us in a great place for the data that's coming next.

My customer solved the issue by using ServicePrincipalCredentials.

Plus, thanks for sharing the information. I understood that we need to acquire token from batch endpoint "https://batch.core.windows.net/".

My customer had a trouble because he didn't set resource = "https://batch.core.windows.net/" ,so the default resource "https://login.microsoftonline.com/" was set and his token was not for batch account.

```
from azure.batch import BatchServiceClient
from azure.common.credentials import ServicePrincipalCredentials

credentials = ServicePrincipalCredentials(
    client_id=CLIENT_ID,
    secret=SECRET,
    tenant=TENANT_ID,
    resource="https://batch.core.windows.net/"
)
    batch_client = BatchServiceClient(
    credentials,
    base_url=BATCH_ACCOUNT_URL
)
```
Now the problem is solved. Thanks so much for your kind help

aiohttp cannot just use `http.cookiejar.Cookie` -- it has incompatible interface with `http.cookies.SimpleCookie`.
`rfc2965` is obsoleted by [RFC6265](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6265) BTW.
The RFC explicitly enumerates all allowed cookie properties (and header name is `Set-Cookie`, `Set-Cookie2` is obsolete).
The list of properties is fixed. For `SameSite` where is a new [RFC draft](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-west-first-party-cookies-07)  but it is not accepted yet.

You can address this issue by your self just building your own URL and not using the params kw, for example

```python
from urllib.parse import urlencode, urlsplit, urlunsplit, SplitResult

def merge_params(url, params):
    """ Return a new URL composed by the `url` plus the
    params.
    """
    u = urlsplit(url)

    if u.query:
        qs = "{}&{}".format(u.query, urlencode(params))
    else:
        qs = urlencode(params)

    new = SplitResult(
        scheme=u.scheme,
        netloc=u.netloc,
        path=u.path,
        query=qs,
        fragment=u.fragment
    )

    return urlunsplit(new)
```

I disagree. Updating for key `'1'` means _updating_ existing key-value pair (not deleting it and adding new one). Please note, that this is not implementation detail point of view, but it is how end user see and use it.

Please also compare multidict ordering with: `collections.OrderedDict` and new Python 3.6 "compact" `dict` implementation. Both preserve insertion key order on update.

Now multidict behavior is _last update preserving order_ not _insertion preserving order_ and if you decide to leave this behavior, this difference should be clearly stated documentation. Now it is confusing for user  familiar with `OrderedDict` or "compact" `dict`, because multidict ordering behavior is different from ordering behavior in these objects from standard library.

### Two solutions

#### 1 not general
I happened to find that changing the target to `D:\Anaconda3\python.exe "D:/Anaconda3/Scripts/jupyter-notebook-script.py" --notebook-dir="D:\Documents\Jupyter"` instead could solve this problem.
But it might not be a general solution for those who want to specify interpreter or virtualenv.

#### 2 recommended
Thus I tried another solution. Setting `c.NotebookApp.notebook_dir =` in `jupyter_notebook_config.py` instead of adding `--notebook-dir=` in the shortcut target, and then 'Download As Python' worked. I did not need to modify the original shortcut anymore.

### A guess
So I suppose, adding `--notebook-dir=` argument to the original shortcut to specify startup notebook directory may lead to some unexpected mistakes.

Regarding discussion in #445, we should have the message be a string to keep compatibility with default Exceptions (and [PEP-352](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0352/) ), and add a `.errors` attribute to hang the parseable info from.

eg:
```
class BaseError(Exception):
    def __init__(self, message, errors=None):

        # Call the base class constructor with the parameters it needs
        super(BaseError, self).__init__(message)

        # Now for your custom code...
        self.errors = errors
```

PEP-352 excerpt:
> No restriction is placed upon what may be passed in for args for backwards-compatibility reasons. In practice, though, only a single string argument should be used.

What I found out:
* The tool does not take care of dependencies, which is okay because each repo does a pretty good job of reporting it's own dependencies. It means "development mode" is not exceptional but it is a typical part of bootstrapping. Why isn't this the 'only' mode?

* I had to make some changes to the repositories which were already fixed on master due to the compiler being a more recent one. This is a normal part of bootstrapping.

`tmux` AFAIK does not save environment it starts with side from the very first run: when server is started it does inherit parent environment. All other `tmux` runs it merely directs tmux server what to do, then tmux *server* forks, obviously having child gain server’s environment (not sure what stopped that other tmux runs from just dumping all environment on server to be used in child later: this works fine with even powerline-daemon which ought to run on each prompt). You *can* write a plugin for the *shell* run inside a client which will notify about environment changes, powerline actually does that for current working directory, but this approach is far from universal and trying to use the existing code for all of the environment present in shell is going to bite performance hard and create lots of environment variables in tmux which is not healthy.

Yes, I'm clear. What I was asking about was that since the only difference between the existing single diode model (SDM) and a model that included the [thin film recombination loss current](http://files.pvsyst.com/help/thinfilm_recombinationloss.htm) is the term:

    I_rec = I_L * d2_mu_tau / (V_bi - V_diode)

then:

* does it really make sense to create a separate `sdm_thin_film` method that is nearly identical to the existing SDM method?
* would it be possible to just include `I_rec = I_L * d2_mu_tau / (V_bi - V_diode)` into the existing `pvsystem.singlediode` and just turn it on as needed?

As I said previously, the solution for either is exactly the same: specify a bunch of diode voltages, solve for cell current then back calculate the cell voltage. For specific voltages and max power, then you can either interpolate, use Newton, or use a bisection search method. (Maybe Lambert-W also works but I'm not as familiar with that formulation?)

@wintersteiger Another way to handle this (which is what I did when the CMake build system was first being developed) is to simply have the Z3 repository cloned twice. I exchanged changes between the copies by adding a local remote.

```
# Make two copies
$ git clone git@github.com:Z3Prover/z3.git copy1
# Reference is just to save disk space. You can omit it if you want
$ git clone git@github.com:Z3Prover/z3.git --reference ./copy1 copy2

# Add a remote named "copy2" to the "copy1" repo so it can fetch/pull from it
$ cd copy1
$ git remote add copy2 $(realpath ../copy2)
$ git remote -v
copy2	/home/dan/dev/z3/XXX/copy2 (fetch)
copy2	/home/dan/dev/z3/XXX/copy2 (push)
origin	git@github.com:Z3Prover/z3.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:Z3Prover/z3.git (push)
# We can now do `git fetch copy2` and `git pull copy2 <branch_name>`


# Add a remote named "copy1" to the "copy2" repo so it can fetch/pull from it
$ cd ../copy2
$ git remote add copy1 $(realpath ../copy1)
copy1	/home/dan/dev/z3/XXX/copy1 (fetch)
copy1	/home/dan/dev/z3/XXX/copy1 (push)
origin	git@github.com:Z3Prover/z3.git (fetch)
origin	git@github.com:Z3Prover/z3.git (push)
# We can now do `git fetch copy1` and `git pull copy1 <branch_name>`
```

Note you can't push to "copy1" or "copy2" but you can fetch from it. This is useful if you want to keep local only changes sync'ed between the repositories.

Looking at the stacktrace further, it looks like a Unicode/byte string
problem. The Python version is looking for a byte string "message" and I
think the node version is sending a Unicode string.

The problems comes from the fact that at some point msg pack had a single
"blob of bytes" type. Which was used for utf8 or byte strings. Now msgpack
handles the be difference between raw string and utf8 strings.

In python3 there is a strong distinction between the two types as well. And
here we are.

From what I understand about deadlocks in the official documentation, this happens when two transactions acquire locks on different objects in different order. 

Things that might be worth looking at:
* where is `cache.get()` used? Appart from pyfxa?
* In the cache backend code, the deletion of expired rows and the select are done in two separate instructions (both are executed in the same transaction, since in the same `with connect()`)

Just to check the kind of lock, in one terminal I ran:

```
[dbname] # BEGIN;
BEGIN
Time: 0,823 ms
[dbname] # DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl;
DELETE 0
Time: 23,002 ms
[dbname] # 

```

And in the other one ([source](https://wiki.postgresql.org/wiki/Lock_Monitoring))
```sql
 SELECT a.datname,
         c.relname,
         l.transactionid,
         l.mode,
         l.GRANTED,
         a.usename,
         a.query, 
         a.query_start,
         age(now(), a.query_start) AS "age", 
         a.pid 
    FROM  pg_stat_activity AS a
     JOIN pg_locks         l ON l.pid = a.pid
     JOIN pg_class         c ON c.oid = l.relation
    ORDER BY a.query_start;
```

And we can see that the `DELETE FROM cache` takes a table level lock:

```
-[ RECORD 1 ]-+---------------------------------------------------------
datname       | dbname
relname       | idx_cache_ttl
transactionid | ¤
mode          | RowExclusiveLock
granted       | t
usename       | postgres

query_start   | 2017-08-08 09:08:47.311075+00
age           | 00:02:04.741193
pid           | 29603
-[ RECORD 2 ]-+---------------------------------------------------------
datname       | dbname
relname       | cache_pkey
transactionid | ¤
mode          | RowExclusiveLock
granted       | t
usename       | postgres
query         | DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl;
query_start   | 2017-08-08 09:08:47.311075+00
age           | 00:02:04.741193
pid           | 29603
-[ RECORD 3 ]-+---------------------------------------------------------
datname       | dbname
relname       | cache
transactionid | ¤
mode          | RowExclusiveLock
granted       | t
usename       | postgres
query         | DELETE FROM cache WHERE ttl IS NOT NULL AND now() > ttl;
query_start   | 2017-08-08 09:08:47.311075+00
age           | 00:02:04.741193
pid           | 29603

```

> the way it has been implemented in Postgresql makes it difficult to apply to no SQL backends

Which leads to the question: why do you want a No SQL backend ?

> What you call memory-to-disk is exactly what redis is offering so I would be in favor of keeping Redis rather than replacing it by another backend.

What I call a memory-to-disk backend is a toy implementation in python so Kinto "works" just to try it out on a single process for fun - rather than having to install and run Redis.

> <troll>I don't think we can say that our experience with Hello led us to some problems, we already had 
more problem with Kinto and Postgresql than we had for 2 years of Hello.</troll>

*cough* *cough*   Choosing Redis over a classical database in Hello was a very poor choice. We had to deploy HUGE servers to make sure that we had enough room to fit the whole database in RAM, and then worry a lot about sharding and replication. If we had picked Postgres or MySQL from day one we woud have avoided a lot of trouble. This was just to have the TTL feature, but that cost us a lot more work than if we have had crons to clean up the DB.  We were just lucky the DB did grow more ;)

Redis is a cache server, not a real RDBMS - I think you need to admit it and stop trying to use it for everything ;)

The way the update_record works is that it will only update a record for a particular specimen if the following three conditions are met:

conditions are: 
aniso_s is not null
aniso_type == type
specimen == specimen # name is correct

In http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35164529/in-tensorflow-is-there-any-way-to-just-initialize-uninitialised-variables I found the following workaround. Although variables coming from the Adam-Optimizer are also not initialized some variables such as `Training_step` come from tflearn. The workaround feels a bit hacky, is it possible to initialize then inside tflearn?

```python
...
with tf.Session() as sess:

    # Store all variables
    temp = set(tf.all_variables())

    # New variables are added in these operations, which are not initialized
    trainop = tflearn.TrainOp(loss=cost, metric=None, optimizer=optimizer, batch_size=batch_size)
    trainer = tflearn.Trainer(train_ops=trainop, tensorboard_verbose=0, session=sess)

    # Initializing the new variables
    uninititalized = set(tf.all_variables()) - temp
    print('The Following Variables have been uninitialized {}'.format([u.name for u in uninititalized]))
    sess.run(tf.initialize_variables(uninititalized))


    trainer.fit({x: X, y: Y}, n_epoch=10, show_metric=False, run_id='Big_Run_1')
```

There are two sides the the conversion.  `load` handles populating a local `MatrixXd value` from a python value, and `cast` handles populating a python value from a local `MatrixXd`.  We're using Eigen::Map internally now, but only to tell Eigen how to copy the data into a fundamental Eigen type.

So, right now, copying is being done in *both* cases.

Some thoughts on this: We'd need another type_caster, (perhaps for an Eigen::Map type?), to handle doing this without copying.  I think there are two potential options: one for making Eigen deal with numpy data, and one for making numpy deal with Eigen data.  (This might also get more complicated, i.e. with trying to make numpy look at non-uniform Eigen data and vice versa--but that's probably an acceptable limitation).  I'll look into it, but it'll be a few days before I can do anything on it (so feel free to write something in a PR before then if you feel so inclined).

One thing that I have thought about a few times, but never gotten around to implementing, is a `py::type` class to encapsulate type objects.  There are a few internal places currently where pybind deals with `PyTypeObject *`s directly which would be a bit cleaner with an encapsulation class along the lines of `py::object`.

It would fit in between `py::object` and `py::class_<>`, and would be constructible.  With some implicit conversions and perhaps minor API additions (i.e. to accept the new type object in `isinstance`) I think you could get essentially what you're looking for, but keep the Python C API isolated from the exposed pybind11 Python C++ API.
Running the interpreter from the C++ side is not an officially supported feature, so I fear that you are on your own. That said, I would be curious to know why it fails if you do find out.

This is a duplicate of https://github.com/certbot/certbot/issues/5157

The error is with the python path and the use of `dist-packages`. We can add a path configuration, e.g., `dist.pth`, to `/opt/eff.org/certbot/venv/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/dist.pth`. However, `pth` files are only processed if they are in a site directory (e.g., `site-packages`) and `pth` files in the newly appointed path (i.e., dist-packages) will not be read. We create a sitecustomize.py in `/opt/eff.org/certbot/venv/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/` with the following: 

```
import site
site.addsitedir('/opt/eff.org/certbot/venv/lib64/python2.7/dist-packages')
```

The `sitecustomize.py` sets dist-packages as a site directory and will successfully read the `zope.interface-4.4.3-py2.7-nspkg.pth`. This avoids the `from zope.interface import Interface` error `ImportError`.

Further, I found that `/usr/local/bin/certbot-auto` re-creates the virtualenv `venv`. This will result in the same python path error we are trying to avoid. I recommend applying the above and manually sourcing the activate script in `venv`; e.g., `. /opt/eff.org/certbot/venv/bin/activate`. 

I prefer this approach because it avoids bashing over system-level python packages.

Alright, so obviously this PR is a pretty massive change. Some people, naturally, won't like it due to the way it mocks up the discord.py commands extension. So, I'm going to lay out some reasons why it's a positive and necessary change, since that seems to be missing from this PR conversation.

### Abstract
Changing locale at runtime is not a problem for strings passed into their `_()` function, as long as said function is called every time that string is needed. However, due to the discord.py convention of having command and cog help being located in docstrings (a convention which should not be broken), translating these strings at runtime becomes a more complex issue.

In order to solve the problem with commands, we need to have a better understanding of how discord.py manages command help after the command is instantiated, and how the help formatter retrieves it. The default `discord.ext.commands.Command` object has just one relevant attribute here: `help`, which is simply assigned the value of the `help` kwarg if it exists, else it is assigned a *cleaned up* original async function's docstring. The help formatter then simply uses this `help` attribute in the embed which is sent to the user.

From here, I believe we have two options: translate the command help from the help formatter and leave the `Command` object as is, or subclass the `Command` object so that the translation occurs internally.

### Help formatter method
Doing translation within the help formatter would mean very minimal changes would need to be made to cogs. Perhaps an extra import and an extra decorator would be all it takes to make command help translatable at runtime. However, the help formatter itself would need to do some funky things to be able to do the translation. The original docstring of the callback would need to be accessed and the `help` attribute of the `Command` would be ignored. The help formatter would also need to find the `_()` function for this cog. This sort of operation, in my opinion, should be done internally by the `Command` object.. Not only this, but it complicates the writing of custom help formatters for Red. Also, should the help formatter have the responsibility of translation? It is a *formatter* after all.

### Subclassing method
This method is the one implemented in this PR. In most cases, updating a cog to command help translatable would be as simple as changing the line `from discord.ext import commands` to `from redbot.core import commands`, and adding a single decorator to the cog class. The `help` attribute has become a `@property`, and this is where the translation is done.

However I didn't just see this method as a solution, but as an opportunity. Structuring the imports and core `commands` package like this makes future subclassing and overriding of `discord.ext.commands` attributes more than trivial. The class can simply be added as an attribute to `redbot.core.commands`, and existing cogs needn't even be modified to be using the new object.

### Overriding `discord.ext.commands`
So, as this PR states, the new `redbot.core.commands` module acts as a complete substitute for importing `discord.ext.commands`. The `redbot.core.commands` module simply imports everything attached to `discord.ext.commands`, then overrides any imports which we have subclassed. I think this is what's really making people apprehensive about this PR, however it would be nice to get some more specific thoughts. :)

Uh.... No, it looks like a silly mistake on my part. I think that assertion should just be deleted. (And also the next one, the `assert lock.locked()` – that one should pass at least because it's vacuously true, but it's not making the example any clearer.) Good catch, thanks!

I wonder if there's a better example – that one's pretty subtle. I think it's there because it was literally the problem that pushed me to implement `wait_all_tasks_blocked`, but since then I've started using it all over the place...

> there is a race condition between process creation and opening a monitor. If the process has already terminated we get an exception, or we might even monitor the wrong process. I'm not sure this is relevant to this issue

So this is one of those classic weird and subtle Unix things. You've heard of "zombie processes"? On Unix when a process exits, the kernel cleans up most of it (the address space, the threads, etc.), but it keeps around a little bit of metadata (the process name, the return value) – this is a "zombie process". This metadata *stays in the process table under the original process's PID*, until someone calls `waitpid`, which consumes the zombie (om nom nom) and frees up that slot in the process table.

So after you fork a child process, the pid you get back is guaranteed to keep referring to that exact process – or at least, its undead remains – until you call `waitpid`. This avoids the race condition. (Unless someone else calls `waitpid` on our process without our knowledge, of course, but hey, that's life.)

This does mean that you have to be careful that for each child process, you call `waitpid` exactly once, and that once you've called `waitpid` you can't use `EVFILT_PROC`. But that's a concern for higher-level code to worry about, not something that we need handle down here in the low-level kevent API.

It would be neat to have a built in randomized/flakey-network mode; think like a 1 button hypothesis test suite for network protocol annoyances.

Possibly this might work like: run the code once to calibrate how much communication happens, and then rerun N times with random delays injected, or if more ambitious and requested, with network failures.

One might want to have ways to tune the kind and distribution of random delays (breaking up data by introducing a virtual delay after every byte is a good stress test, but might be too slow for larger tests). Some kind of hypothesis-like tuning towards "weird" patterns would also be good (e.g. adding tons of delays on one connection but not another, or on one direction but not another).

It would also be neat if there were a way to write down a pattern of injected faults, to extract repeatable regression test cases from a randomized run, and potentially to allow particular fault patterns or fault pattern generators to be written by hand.

I've made this explicit since at the time the feature was developed it wasn't quite clear what modes users would use besides run from the last available checkpoint. Then there's the question of multiple workflows in the same folder and disambiguation of which checkpoint to use.
